goal: Complete Cortex Phase 2 — automation, intelligence, and polish that makes it work without manual tool calls, easy enough that any Claude Code user would want to install it.

features.name: Project scaffolding and SQLite schema
features.description: Set up Python project (pyproject.toml, src/cortex/, tests/). Create the full SQLite schema.
acceptance_criteria: Running pip install -e . from project root succeeds
Importing from cortex.db import init_db works and creates a .db file with all tables
curated_memories table has correct columns and CHECK constraint
curated_memories_fts virtual table exists and indexes content + tags
raw_chunks table has correct columns
meta table exists with key-value structure
WAL mode is enabled on the database
pytest runs with 0 errors

features.name: Curated layer: remember and FTS5 search
features.description: Implement the curated memory layer. remember(content, type, source, tags, confidence) inserts into curated_memories and syncs to the FTS5 index. recall_curated(query, type, limit) searches via FTS5 BM25 ranking. Results include the BM25 rank score. Soft delete and superseded memories excluded from search by default.
acceptance_criteria: remember('Dan prefers SQLite over Postgres', type='preference', source='manual') returns an integer ID
recall_curated('database preference') returns the stored memory with a rank score
recall_curated with type filter only returns memories of that type
recall_curated with limit=1 returns exactly 1 result
Soft-deleted memories (deleted_at set) do not appear in recall results
FTS5 search handles partial word matches via prefix queries
Results are returned as dicts with keys: id, content, type, source, tags, confidence, rank, created_at

features.name: Embedding engine with fastembed
features.description: Create embeddings module wrapping fastembed with bge-small-en-v1.5. Provide embed_one(text) -> list[float] and embed_batch(texts) -> list[list[float]]. Model loaded lazily on first call. Include serialize/deserialize helpers for SQLite BLOB storage. No PyTorch dependency.
acceptance_criteria: embed_one('hello world') returns a list of 384 floats
embed_batch(['a', 'b', 'c']) returns 3 vectors, each 384 floats
Round-tripping through serialize/deserialize produces identical vectors
Model is NOT loaded until the first embed call (lazy init)
No PyTorch in dependencies — only fastembed + onnxruntime

features.name: Raw layer: chunk storage and vector search
features.description: Implement the raw chunks layer. store_chunk(content, source, source_type, metadata) embeds the content, stores in raw_chunks with embedding BLOB, and indexes in sqlite-vec virtual table. recall_raw(query, source_type, limit) embeds the query, performs cosine similarity search via sqlite-vec, and returns ranked results.
acceptance_criteria: store_chunk stores content with embedding and returns an ID
recall_raw('database preference') finds semantically similar chunks
recall_raw with source_type filter only returns matching source types
recall_raw respects the limit parameter
Semantic search works: store 'I love pizza', search 'favorite food' returns it
sqlite-vec extension loads on the current platform without errors

features.name: Unified recall with layer fallback
features.description: Implement the unified recall(query, type, limit, layer) function. layer='curated' (default) searches FTS5 only. layer='raw' searches vectors only. layer='both' searches curated first, then fills remaining limit from raw, deduplicating.
acceptance_criteria: recall(query) defaults to curated layer search
recall(query, layer='raw') searches only the raw layer
recall(query, layer='both') returns results from both layers, curated first
layer='both' deduplicates results that exist in both layers
Results from both layers have a normalized score for consistent ranking
The simple query expansion splits 'database preference' into multiple search terms

features.name: Forget and supersede operations
features.description: Implement forget(id) for soft-delete and supersede(old_id, new_content) which creates a new curated memory with supersedes_id pointing to old_id. Supersession chains queryable via get_history(id).
acceptance_criteria: forget(id) sets deleted_at and memory no longer appears in recall results
forget does NOT physically delete the row
supersede(old_id, 'new fact') creates a new memory and soft-deletes the old one
The new memory's supersedes_id points to old_id
get_history(new_id) returns the full supersession chain including the old memory
Attempting to forget a non-existent ID raises a clear error

features.name: Confidence decay system
features.description: Implement confidence decay for curated memories. Exponential decay with configurable half-life. Access reinforces confidence.
acceptance_criteria: New memories start with confidence=1.0
Accessing a memory via recall reinforces it (confidence reset to 1.0, updated_at refreshed)
decay_confidence() reduces confidence based on time since updated_at
A memory untouched for 90 days has confidence ~0.5 (half-life)
A memory untouched for 180 days has confidence ~0.25
Memories with confidence < 0.1 are flagged as stale in status output

features.name: Status and health dashboard
features.description: Implement status() returning counts, type/source breakdowns, timestamps, db file size, stale count, integrity checks.
acceptance_criteria: status() returns a dict with keys: curated_count, raw_count, by_type, by_source, last_memory_at, last_consolidation_at, db_size_mb, stale_count
by_type maps memory types to counts
by_source maps source types to counts
stale_count is the number of curated memories with confidence < 0.1
Integrity check reports if FTS5 count differs from curated_memories count
Integrity check reports if vec index count differs from raw_chunks count

features.name: MCP server with 4 core tools
features.description: Create the MCP server using FastMCP. Expose exactly 4 tools: remember, recall, forget, status. Support stdio + SSE transport. CORTEX_DB_PATH env var.
acceptance_criteria: Running python -m cortex.server starts an MCP server on stdio transport
Running python -m cortex.server --transport sse --port 8765 starts SSE server
The server exposes exactly 4 tools: remember, recall, forget, status
remember tool accepts content, type, tags, layer parameters
recall tool accepts query, type, limit, layer parameters
forget tool accepts id and returns confirmation
status tool returns the full health dashboard
CORTEX_DB_PATH environment variable controls database location

features.name: Text chunking and raw layer ingestion pipeline
features.description: Create ingestion module for bulk content. chunk_text splits text into overlapping chunks. ingest_file reads, chunks, embeds, and stores. CLI entry point.
acceptance_criteria: chunk_text splits a 1000-word text into ~3 chunks of ~300 tokens each with overlap
ingest_file stores all chunks with embeddings in raw_chunks
Session log ingestion skips tool call messages and messages under 50 chars
CLI accepts a file path and source_type flag
Ingestion is idempotent — re-running on same file doesn't create duplicates
A summary is printed: N chunks ingested, M skipped

features.name: Extraction pipeline: raw to curated
features.description: Create extraction pipeline that promotes raw chunks to curated memories via Haiku prompts.
acceptance_criteria: extract_prompt generates a valid prompt listing raw chunks with IDs
The prompt instructs the LLM to output JSON with typed memories
process_extraction creates curated memories and extraction links
Each extraction links a raw_chunk_id to a curated_memory_id
CLI without --process outputs prompt to stdout
CLI with --process reads JSON from stdin and creates memories
Already-extracted chunks are skipped

features.name: MEMORY.md migration ingestion
features.description: One-time migration tool that reads existing MEMORY.md files and imports entries into curated layer.
acceptance_criteria: Running on Dan's actual MEMORY.md imports all entries as curated memories
Project entries get type='entity', User entries get type='preference', Feedback entries get type='procedure', Reference entries get type='fact'
Source is set to 'memory_md_migration' for all entries
Idempotent — running twice doesn't create duplicates
A summary is printed: N memories imported, M skipped (duplicates)

features.name: End-to-end test suite
features.description: Comprehensive pytest suite covering both layers and all operations.
acceptance_criteria: pytest runs all tests with 0 failures
Tests cover: remember, recall_curated, recall_raw, recall (unified), forget, supersede, get_history, decay_confidence, status
At least one test verifies FTS5 search ranks relevant results above irrelevant ones (30+ memories)
At least one test verifies vector search ranks semantically similar content higher (20+ chunks)
Tests use temporary databases (no leftover files)
Confidence decay math is tested: half-life produces expected values
MCP server can be instantiated without errors in tests

features.name: Graceful degradation and production hardening
features.description: Add graceful degradation if sqlite-vec/fastembed unavailable. Logging. CLI dispatcher. Error handling.
acceptance_criteria: If sqlite-vec is unavailable, curated layer still works and recall(layer='raw') returns a clear error message instead of crashing
If fastembed is unavailable, recall falls back to FTS5-only search with a warning
CORTEX_LOG_LEVEL=DEBUG enables verbose logging
CLI python -m cortex dispatches to subcommands: server, ingest, extract, migrate, status
MCP server returns structured error responses for invalid inputs (doesn't crash)
Running python -m cortex --version prints the version

features.name: Stop hook: automatic capture to staging JSONL
features.description: A shell script (~/scripts/cortex-capture.sh) reads the Claude Code Stop hook JSON payload from stdin, extracts the assistant response text, and appends a JSONL line to ~/.cortex/staging/YYYY-MM-DD.jsonl. Must be fast (<100ms) — no embedding, no network, just a file append. The Stop hook entry is added to ~/.claude/settings.json alongside existing hooks.
acceptance_criteria: A script exists at ~/scripts/cortex-capture.sh that reads Stop hook JSON from stdin
The script appends a JSONL line to ~/.cortex/staging/YYYY-MM-DD.jsonl (date from system clock)
Each JSONL line has exactly three fields: timestamp (ISO 8601), session_id, content
The staging directory ~/.cortex/staging/ is created automatically if it does not exist
Responses shorter than 50 characters are skipped silently (no line written)
The Stop hook entry appears in ~/.claude/settings.json under hooks.Stop without overwriting existing entries
Running the script manually with a test JSON payload completes in under 100ms

features.name: Staging file ingestion CLI subcommand
features.description: A new `ingest-staging` subcommand for `python -m cortex`. Reads all JSONL files in a staging directory, chunks each line's content, embeds, and stores in the raw layer. Tracks completed files via the meta table so re-running is idempotent. A companion shell snippet (for the ThinkPad crontab) should rsync staging files from Mac before calling ingest-staging.
acceptance_criteria: `python -m cortex ingest-staging --db <path> --staging-dir <dir>` processes all .jsonl files in the directory
Each JSONL line's content field is chunked and stored in raw_chunks with source_type='session'
Completed staging files are recorded in the meta table (key='ingested_staging_file:<filename>', value='done') to prevent duplicate ingestion
Re-running on already-ingested files skips them and reports N skipped
A summary line is printed: 'N lines processed, M chunks stored, K files completed, J files skipped'
The subcommand is registered in src/cortex/__main__.py dispatch table
A test covers idempotency: ingest same staging file twice, verify chunk count unchanged

features.name: Session-start memory injection hook
features.description:
A SessionStart hook script (~/scripts/cortex-session-start.sh) that queries the Cortex MCP server's SSE HTTP endpoint, retrieves a brief summary of high-confidence recent memories, and prints it to stdout so Claude Code displays it at session start. Fails silently if the server is unreachable. Must complete within 2 seconds.

FAIL FEEDBACK (sprint 3): Two bugs. (1) out=$(ssh ...) & backgrounds the subshell — out never propagates. Write SSH output to a temp file instead. (2) Script calls cortex_status.status(db_path=...) but the actual signature is status(conn: sqlite3.Connection). Fix: ssh thinkpad python3 -c "import sqlite3,json; from cortex.status import status; conn=sqlite3.connect("/home/dan/.cortex/cortex.db"); print(json.dumps(status(conn)))" — or simpler: just use python -m cortex status --db /home/dan/.cortex/cortex.db on the ThinkPad and parse stdout.
acceptance_criteria: A script exists at ~/scripts/cortex-session-start.sh
The script calls the Cortex status endpoint (http://100.108.198.107:8765) with a 2-second timeout
On success, the script prints a one-line summary: 'Cortex: N memories, last updated X ago'
If the server is unreachable or the request times out, the script exits 0 with no output (fails silently)
The SessionStart hook entry appears in ~/.claude/settings.json under hooks.SessionStart without overwriting existing entries
The script completes (success or failure) in under 2 seconds

features.name: Supersede MCP tool and proactive CLAUDE.md instructions
features.description: Expose supersede() as a 5th MCP tool in server.py. Also create a CLAUDE.md at the project root with instructions for Claude to proactively use Cortex tools.
acceptance_criteria: server.py exposes exactly 5 tools: remember, recall, forget, supersede, status
The supersede MCP tool accepts old_id (int), new_content (str), and optional type (str)
Calling supersede soft-deletes the old memory and creates a new one with supersedes_id set
supersede returns {old_id, new_id, status: 'superseded'}
A CLAUDE.md exists at /Users/danielvanstrien/Projects/cortex/CLAUDE.md

CLAUDE.md instructs Claude to: (a) call remember for decisions/preferences/facts, (b) call recall before answering questions about user history, (c) call supersede when information changes, (d) call status periodically
A test in tests/test_server.py covers the supersede tool end-to-end

features.name: Reflect pipeline: insight synthesis from curated memories
features.description: A new `reflect` subcommand for `python -m cortex`. Groups curated memories by type/topic, generates a Haiku prompt to synthesize patterns into higher-level insight memories, and writes them back with links to source IDs.
acceptance_criteria: `python -m cortex reflect --db <path>` generates a reflection prompt and prints it to stdout
The prompt groups curated memories by type and asks the LLM to identify patterns worth remembering as insights
`python -m cortex reflect --db <path> --process` reads JSON from stdin and creates curated memories with type='insight'
Each insight memory stores source_ids (the IDs of memories it was synthesized from) in its content or via the extractions table
Already-reflected memory IDs are tracked in the meta table so they are not re-included in future reflection batches
The subcommand is registered in src/cortex/__main__.py
A test verifies: store 3 related memories, run reflect prompt generation, confirm they appear in the prompt output

features.name: Contradiction detection in extraction pipeline
features.description: Enhance extract.py so the extraction prompt includes the top-5 most similar existing curated memories. The LLM output gains an optional 'supersedes' field. process_extraction calls supersede() when present.
acceptance_criteria: The extraction prompt includes a section 'EXISTING RELATED MEMORIES:' listing up to 5 similar curated memories with their IDs
Each existing memory is labeled '[EXISTING #<id>] <content>'
The extraction output JSON format supports an optional supersedes field: {raw_chunk_ids, content, type, supersedes?: int}
When supersedes is present and non-null, process_extraction calls curated.supersede() instead of remember()
A test covers the happy path: store 'Dan prefers Postgres', ingest a chunk saying 'Dan switched to SQLite exclusively', run extraction, verify the old memory is soft-deleted
If no existing memories are similar (below threshold), the EXISTING section is omitted from the prompt

features.name: One-command installer
features.description: `python -m cortex install` sets up everything for a new user: creates ~/.cortex/, initializes DB, adds Stop hook and SessionStart hook to ~/.claude/settings.json, adds Cortex MCP entry to ~/.claude/.mcp.json. Non-destructive — existing config preserved.
acceptance_criteria: `python -m cortex install` runs to completion without errors
The command creates ~/.cortex/ and initializes the SQLite DB at ~/.cortex/cortex.db
The Stop hook for cortex-capture.sh is added to ~/.claude/settings.json without overwriting existing hooks
The SessionStart hook for cortex-session-start.sh is added without overwriting existing hooks
The Cortex SSE MCP entry is added to ~/.claude/.mcp.json without overwriting other MCP servers
Running install a second time is idempotent — detects existing entries and skips with 'already installed' message
The installer prints a clear step-by-step log of what it did and what it skipped

features.name: Memory CLI browser: list, search, show
features.description: Three new subcommands: `list` shows recent/filtered memories as a table, `search` runs FTS5 search with scores, `show` displays a single memory with its full supersession history.
acceptance_criteria: `python -m cortex list` prints the 20 most recent curated memories as a numbered table (id, type, confidence, snippet)
`python -m cortex list --type fact --limit 10` filters by type and limits output
`python -m cortex search <query>` runs recall_curated and prints ranked results with scores
`python -m cortex show <id>` prints full content, metadata, and supersession chain for the given ID
All three commands accept --db <path>
Output is human-readable plain text, no raw JSON blobs
All three subcommands are registered in src/cortex/__main__.py

features.name: Memory export and import
features.description: `python -m cortex export` dumps all non-deleted curated memories as a JSON array to stdout. `python -m cortex import` reads that JSON from stdin and inserts new memories idempotently.
acceptance_criteria: `python -m cortex export --db <path>` prints a JSON array of all non-deleted curated memories to stdout
Each exported object includes: id, content, type, source, tags, confidence, created_at, supersedes_id
`python -m cortex import --db <path> < memories.json` reads the JSON array and inserts each memory
Import is idempotent: re-importing the same export produces no duplicates
Import prints a summary: N memories imported, M already present (skipped)
Both subcommands are registered in src/cortex/__main__.py
A round-trip test: export from DB A, import into DB B, verify count and content match

features.name: Release packaging and pyproject.toml polish
features.description: Make Cortex installable via `pip install git+https://...`. Pin core dependencies, add [project.scripts] entry points, ensure __version__ is set, add mcp and anthropic-sdk to deps.
acceptance_criteria: pyproject.toml has [project.scripts] entry: cortex = 'cortex.__main__:main'
All direct runtime dependencies are in [project.dependencies] with minimum version pins
cortex.__version__ is set (e.g. '0.2.0') and importable
`python -m cortex --version` prints the version string
Running `pip install -e .` in a fresh venv succeeds with no missing-dependency errors
Running `python -m cortex status` after a fresh-venv install works (DB created on demand)
fastmcp or mcp is listed as a dependency with a version pin

features.name: README and install guide
features.description: README.md at the project root covering: what Cortex does, 5-minute setup guide, comparison table vs. manual memory, ASCII architecture diagram, and usage examples for each MCP tool and CLI subcommand.
acceptance_criteria: README.md exists at /Users/danielvanstrien/Projects/cortex/README.md
README includes a '5-minute setup' section with numbered steps a non-programmer can follow

README includes an ASCII architecture diagram showing the full pipeline: Mac Stop hook → staging JSONL → rsync → ThinkPad → ingest-staging → raw_chunks → extract → curated_memories → MCP server → Claude Code
README includes a comparison table: 'Without Cortex' vs 'With Cortex' for at least 4 scenarios
README includes usage examples for all 5 MCP tools (remember, recall, forget, supersede, status)
README includes usage examples for key CLI subcommands: ingest, ingest-staging, extract, reflect, list, search, show, export, import, install, doctor
README is written at a level a non-programmer can understand

features.name: cortex doctor: health checker and diagnostics
features.description: `python -m cortex doctor` runs a full end-to-end diagnostics check and prints a pass/fail checklist. Checks DB, FTS5, sqlite-vec, fastembed, staging directory, and optionally the ThinkPad MCP server via SSH.
acceptance_criteria: `python -m cortex doctor` prints a checklist of checks, each marked PASS, FAIL, or WARN
Checks include: DB file exists and opens, FTS5 index intact, sqlite-vec loads, fastembed available, staging directory writable
Each FAIL includes a one-line remediation hint (e.g. 'Run python -m cortex install')
The command exits with code 0 if all checks pass, code 1 if any FAIL
If --remote <ssh-host> is given, doctor also checks: MCP server running on port 8765, cron jobs exist in remote crontab
WARN (not FAIL) for optional components: sqlite-vec, fastembed, remote MCP server
The subcommand is registered in src/cortex/__main__.py
